title: Amharic Sounds
description: Learn the unique sounds of Amharic including vowels, consonants, and ejectives

steps.title: The Amharic Sound System
steps.body: Amharic has 7 vowels and about 31 consonants. Many sounds are similar to English, but some are unique. This lesson focuses on pronunciation since we are learning to SPEAK.

steps.title: The 7 Vowels
steps.body: Amharic vowels: a (as in 'bar'), u (as in 'boot'), i (as in 'seat'), a (as in 'bar'), e (as in 'way'), ə (the schwa - like 'e' in 'roses', the hardest vowel for English speakers), o (as in 'boat').

steps.title: Ejective Consonants
steps.body: Amharic has 5 ejective consonants with no English equivalent: t' (sharp t), k'/q (sharp k), ch' (sharp ch), ts'/s' (sharp ts), p' (sharp p). These are made by building air pressure in the throat and releasing it sharply.

steps.title: Gemination
steps.body: Doubling a consonant changes meaning! 'ale' means 'he said' but 'alle' means 'there is'. 'meta' means 'he came' but 'metta' means 'he hit'. Always listen for doubled consonants.

title: Greetings & Farewells
description: Essential greetings, farewells, and polite expressions for daily use

steps.title: Saying Hello
steps.body: The most common greeting is 'selam' (ሰላም) - informal hello. For formal situations, use 'tena yistilign' (ጤና ይስጥልኝ), which literally means 'may health be given to me'.

steps.title: How Are You?
steps.body: In Amharic, you must know if you're speaking to a man or woman. To a man: 'indet neh?' To a woman: 'indet nesh?' The answer: 'dehna negn' (I am fine).

steps.title: Saying Goodbye
steps.body: To a man: 'dehna hun' (stay well). To a woman: 'dehna hugni'. Informally, you can say 'chaw' (borrowed from Italian 'ciao').

steps.title: Other Essentials
steps.body: 'Ameseginalehu' means 'thank you'. 'Yiqirta' means 'excuse me/sorry'. 'Awo' means 'yes', 'aye' means 'no', and 'ishi' means 'OK/alright'.

title: Pronouns & "To Be"
description: Learn personal pronouns and the verb 'to be' in all its forms

steps.title: Personal Pronouns
steps.body: Amharic pronouns: ine (እኔ - I), ante (አንተ - you m), anchi (አንቲ - you f), irswo (እርስዎ - you formal), issu (እሱ - he), isswa (እሷ - she), igna (እኛ - we), innante (እናንተ - you pl), innessu (እነሱ - they).

steps.title: The Verb 'To Be'
steps.body: The verb 'to be' uses special forms: negn (I am), neh (you are m), nesh (you are f), new (he is), nat (she is), nen (we are), nachihu (you pl are), nachew (they are).

steps.title: Pro-Drop Language
steps.body: Amharic is a pro-drop language, meaning you can drop the pronoun because the verb ending tells you who is speaking. 'Dehna negn' already means 'I am fine' without needing 'ine'.

steps.title: Negative 'To Be'
steps.body: To say 'is not', use 'aydelem' (አይደለም). This form is the same for all persons in casual speech. Example: 'Ine memhir aydelem' (I am not a teacher).

title: Numbers 1-10
description: Learn to count from one to ten in Amharic

steps.title: Numbers 1-5
steps.body: The first five numbers: and (አንድ - 1), hulet (ሁለት - 2), sost (ሶስት - 3), arat (አራት - 4), amist (አምስት - 5). Practice saying each one clearly.

steps.title: Numbers 6-10
steps.body: The next five: sidist (ስድስት - 6), sebat (ሰባት - 7), simint (ስምንት - 8), zetegn (ዘጨኝ - 9), asir (አስር - 10).

steps.title: Practice Counting
steps.body: Try counting from 1 to 10 quickly: and, hulet, sost, arat, amist, sidist, sebat, simint, zetegn, asir. Repeat until it flows naturally.

title: Numbers 11-20
description: Expand your counting skills to twenty using the asra pattern

steps.title: The Asra Pattern
steps.body: Numbers 11-19 follow a simple pattern: asra (አስራ - ten) + the unit digit. So 11 is 'asra and', 12 is 'asra hulet', and so on. This is much simpler than English!

steps.title: Numbers 11-15
steps.body: asra and (11), asra hulet (12), asra sost (13), asra arat (14), asra amist (15). Just combine 'asra' with the numbers you already know.

steps.title: Numbers 16-20
steps.body: asra sidist (16), asra sebat (17), asra simint (18), asra zetegn (19). Twenty is a unique word: haya (ሃያ). It does not follow the asra pattern.

title: Family Members
description: Learn the words for family members and close relatives

steps.title: Parents & Grandparents
steps.body: abat (አባት - father), inat (እናት - mother), ayat (አያት - grandfather), inist (እንስት - grandmother). Family is central to Ethiopian culture, and these words are used constantly.

steps.title: Siblings
steps.body: wendim (ወንድም - brother), ihit (እህት - sister). For older brother: tiliq wendim. For younger sister: tinish ihit. Birth order matters in Ethiopian families.

steps.title: Spouse & Children
steps.body: bal (ባል - husband), mist (ሚስት - wife), lij (ልጅ - child), wend lij (ወንድ ልጅ - son), set lij (ሴት ልጅ - daughter). 'Beteseb' (ቤተሰብ) means family.

title: Common Nouns Part 1
description: Learn essential everyday nouns and understand noun gender

steps.title: Essential Nouns
steps.body: Every language has core nouns you need daily. In Amharic, nouns have grammatical gender (masculine or feminine) which affects articles and adjectives. Most nouns are masculine by default.

steps.title: Places
steps.body: bet (ቤት - house, m), ketema (ከተማ - city, f), menged (መንገድ - road, m), timhirt bet (ትምህርት ቤት - school), gebeya (ገበያ - market, m).

steps.title: Noun Gender
steps.body: Masculine nouns take 'u' as a definite article suffix: bet→betu (the house). Feminine nouns take 'wa' or 'itu': mekina→mekinawa (the car). Learning gender with each noun saves confusion later.

steps.title: People & Things
steps.body: sew (ሰው - person, m), wuha (ውሃ - water, m), migib (ምግብ - food, m), mekina (መኪና - car, f). Note that 'mekina' is one of the common feminine nouns.

title: Common Nouns Part 2
description: Learn nouns for food, drink, time, and everyday concepts

steps.title: Food & Drink
steps.body: Ethiopia has a rich food culture. Key words: buna (ቡና - coffee, m), shay (ሻይ - tea, m), dabo (ዳቦ - bread, m), injera (እንጀራ - the staple flatbread), wot (ወጥ - stew/sauce).

steps.title: Time-Related Words
steps.body: gize (ጊዜ - time, m), qen (ቀን - day, m), lelit (ሌሊት - night, f), samimt (ሳምንት - week, m), wer (ወር - month, m), amet (አመት - year, m).

steps.title: Money & Work
steps.body: genzeb (ገንዘብ - money, m), sira (ሲራ - work, f), birr (ብር - Ethiopian currency). Ethiopia uses the birr, and knowing money vocabulary is essential for markets and shops.

title: Common Adjectives
description: Learn adjectives for size, quality, color, and description

steps.title: Size & Quality
steps.body: tiliq (ትልቅ - big), tinish (ትንሽ - small), tiru (ጥሩ - good), metfo (መጥፎ - bad), rejim (ረጂም - long/tall), achir (አጭር - short).

steps.title: Descriptive Adjectives
steps.body: addis (አዲስ - new), aroge (አሮገ - old), qonjo (ቅንጆ - beautiful/handsome), feTan (ፈጣን - fast), zegij (ዘገጅ - slow), kebad (ከባድ - heavy).

steps.title: Colors
steps.body: tiqur (ጥቁር - black), nech (ነጭ - white), qey (ቀይ - red), bicha (ቢቻ - yellow), arenguade (አረንጓዴ - green), semayawi (ሰማያዊ - blue).

title: Days of the Week
description: Learn all seven days of the week in Amharic

steps.title: The Seven Days
steps.body: The Ethiopian week starts on Sunday: ihud (እሁድ - Sunday), segnno (ሰኞ - Monday), maksegnno (ማክሰኞ - Tuesday), rebu (ረቡ - Wednesday), hamus (ሐሙስ - Thursday), arb (አርብ - Friday), qidame (ቅዳሜ - Saturday).

steps.title: Using Days in Sentences
steps.body: To say 'on Monday', say 'segnno'. To say 'today is Monday', say 'zare segnno new'. To ask 'what day is it?', say 'zare min qen new?' Days do not need a preposition like 'on' in English.

steps.title: Practice
steps.body: Ethiopian calendars differ from Western ones. The Ethiopian calendar has 13 months and is about 7-8 years behind the Gregorian calendar. But the days of the week are the same cycle of seven.

title: Sentence Structure (SOV)
description: Understand Amharic's Subject-Object-Verb word order

steps.title: SOV Word Order
steps.body: English uses Subject-Verb-Object (I eat food), but Amharic uses Subject-Object-Verb (I food eat). The verb always comes last. This is the most important grammar rule in Amharic.

steps.title: Adjective Placement
steps.body: Adjectives come before the noun they describe, just like English: tiliq bet (big house), tiru sew (good person), addis mekina (new car). This is one area where Amharic and English agree.

steps.title: Building Simple Sentences
steps.body: To build a sentence: put the subject first, then the object, then the verb. 'Ine buna itetallehu' = I coffee drink = I drink coffee. 'Issu migib bela' = He food ate = He ate food.

steps.title: Practice Sentences
steps.body: Try rearranging English sentences into SOV: 'She reads a book' becomes 'isswa metshaf tanebbalech'. Think of the verb as the period at the end of every Amharic sentence.

title: Gender in Amharic
description: Understand how grammatical gender affects speech and grammar

steps.title: Two Genders
steps.body: Amharic has masculine and feminine gender. Every noun is either masculine or feminine. Most nouns are masculine by default. Common feminine nouns include: mekina (car), ketema (city), ager (country), iqist (sister).

steps.title: How Gender Affects Speech
steps.body: When speaking TO someone, verb endings and greetings change based on their gender. 'How are you?' to a man: 'indet neh?' To a woman: 'indet nesh?' This distinction is constant and essential.

steps.title: Gender in Verbs
steps.body: Verb conjugations differ for 'you' masculine vs feminine. 'You went' (m): 'hedk'. 'You went' (f): 'hedsh'. You must always know your listener's gender to conjugate correctly.

steps.title: Common Feminine Nouns
steps.body: While most nouns are masculine, these are feminine: inat (mother), ihit (sister), mist (wife), hager (ሃገር - country), mekina (car), ketema (city), sira (work), igir (እግር - foot).

title: The Verb "To Have"
description: Learn to express possession using the alle construction

steps.title: The Alle Construction
steps.body: Amharic doesn't have a verb 'to have' like English. Instead, it uses 'alle' (አልለ - there is) with pronoun suffixes. Think of 'I have' as 'there is to me' - this is how many Semitic languages work.

steps.title: All Conjugations
steps.body: alleny (አለኝ - I have), alleh (አለህ - you have m), allesh (አለሽ - you have f), allew (አለው - he has), allat (አላት - she has), allen (አለን - we have), allachihu (አላቹሁ - you pl have), allachew (አላቸው - they have).

steps.title: Negative Form
steps.body: The negative of 'alle' is 'yellem' (የለም - there is not). To say 'I don't have': 'yellenyim' (የለኝም). The negative form is built from ye- + l + pronoun + m.

steps.title: Using It in Sentences
steps.body: To say what you have, put the object before the verb: 'mekina alleny' (I have a car), 'genzeb yellenyim' (I don't have money). The thing possessed comes first.

title: Definite Articles & Plurals
description: Learn how to say 'the', 'a', and form plural nouns

steps.title: Definite Article Suffixes
steps.body: Amharic has no separate word for 'the'. Instead, add a suffix: -u/-w for masculine nouns (bet→betu = the house), -wa/-itu for feminine nouns (mekina→mekinawa = the car). The suffix attaches directly to the noun.

steps.title: Indefinite Article
steps.body: For 'a/an', use 'and' (አንድ) before the noun: 'and bet' (a house), 'and sew' (a person). Without any article, the noun is generic: 'bet' can mean 'house' in general.

steps.title: Plural Formation
steps.body: Add '-ochch' (-ዮች) to make nouns plural: bet→betochch (houses), sew→sewochch (people), lij→lijochch (children). This suffix works for nearly all nouns.

steps.title: Definite Plurals
steps.body: To say 'the houses', add the definite suffix to the plural: betochch→betochchu. The pattern is: noun + ochch + u (masculine definite). This combines both plural and definite.

title: Possessives
description: Learn to express ownership with possessive pronouns and suffixes

steps.title: The Ye- Strategy
steps.body: One way to show possession is 'ye-' + pronoun: yene (የኔ - mine/my), yante (የአንተ - yours m), yanchi (የአንቲ - yours f), yessu (የእሱ - his), yesswa (የእሷ - her), yegna (የእኛ - our).

steps.title: Possessive Suffixes
steps.body: You can also attach suffixes directly to the noun: bete (ቤቴ - my house), betih (ቤትህ - your house m), betish (ቤትሽ - your house f), betu (ቤቱ - his house), betwa (ቤቷ - her house).

steps.title: Using Ye- with Names
steps.body: To say someone's possession by name, use 'ye-' + name: 'ye-Abebe bet' (Abebe's house), 'ye-Sara mekina' (Sara's car). This is like English 's but comes before the name.

title: Question Words
description: Learn all essential question words and how to form questions

steps.title: Essential Question Words
steps.body: man (ማን - who), min (ምን - what), yet (የት - where), meche (መቼ - when), lemin (ለምን - why), indet (እንዴት - how), sint (ስንት - how many/how much).

steps.title: Question Word Placement
steps.body: Question words typically go where the answer would be in the sentence, usually before the verb. 'Yet hedeh?' (Where did you go?). 'Min tifelligaleh?' (What do you want?). The word order stays SOV.

steps.title: Yes/No Questions
steps.body: For yes/no questions, just raise your intonation at the end - no extra words needed. 'Hedeh?' (Did you go?) with rising tone. Answer with 'awo' (አዎ - yes) or 'aye/ayleh' (አይ - no).

steps.title: Combining Questions
steps.body: 'Sint new?' (How much is it?). 'Man new?' (Who is it?). 'Min new?' (What is it?). Adding 'new' after a question word is a very common pattern for simple questions.

title: Common Verbs Part 1
description: Learn essential everyday verbs in their infinitive forms

steps.title: Infinitive Forms
steps.body: Amharic infinitives start with 'me-' (መ-). This is like 'to...' in English. mehed (መሄድ - to go), memtat (መምጣት - to come), meblat (መብላት - to eat). Always learn verbs in their infinitive form first.

steps.title: Movement Verbs
steps.body: mehed (መሄድ - to go), memtat (መምጣት - to come), meqemet (መቀመጥ - to stand/stop), mequt (መቁጥ - to sit), merotch (መሮጥ - to run).

steps.title: Essential Daily Verbs
steps.body: meblat (መብላት - to eat), metetat (መጠጣት - to drink), mayet (ማየት - to see), mefeleg (መፈለግ - to want), maseb (ማሰብ - to think), meten (መተኛት - to sleep).

steps.title: Using Infinitives
steps.body: You can use infinitives with 'ifelligallehu' (I want): 'mehed ifelligallehu' (I want to go), 'meblat ifelligallehu' (I want to eat). The infinitive comes before the conjugated verb.

title: Past Tense
description: Learn to conjugate verbs in the past tense using suffix patterns

steps.title: Past Tense Pattern
steps.body: Amharic past tense uses suffixes on the verb stem. Take the infinitive, remove 'me-', and add the appropriate suffix for each person. This is the simplest tense in Amharic.

steps.title: Conjugating Mehed (to go)
steps.body: hedhu (ሄድሁ - I went), hedk (ሄድክ - you went m), hedsh (ሄድሽ - you went f), hede (ሄደ - he went), hedech (ሄደች - she went), hedn (ሄድን - we went), hedachihu (ሄዳቹሁ - you pl went), hedu (ሄዱ - they went).

steps.title: Practice with Meblat (to eat)
steps.body: belahu (I ate), belak (you ate m), belash (you ate f), bela (he ate), belach (she ate), belan (we ate), belu (they ate). The same suffix pattern works for most verbs.

steps.title: Key Suffixes Summary
steps.body: I: -hu, you(m): -k, you(f): -sh, he: -e/-a, she: -ech/-ach, we: -n, you(pl): -achihu, they: -u. Memorize these suffixes and you can conjugate any regular verb.

title: Present/Future Tense
description: Learn the compound imperfect and present progressive forms

steps.title: Compound Imperfect
steps.body: The present/future tense uses a prefix+suffix pattern plus an auxiliary. For 'I go/will go': i- + hed + -allehu = ihedallehu. The prefix changes per person and the auxiliary 'alle' conjugates too.

steps.title: Full Conjugation of Mehed
steps.body: ihedallehu (I go), tihedaleh (you go m), tihediyalesh (you go f), yihedal (he goes), tihedalechi (she goes), inhedaln (we go), tihedalachihu (you pl go), yihedalu (they go).

steps.title: Present Progressive
steps.body: To say 'I am going right now', use the progressive: iyyehedhu new (እየሄድሁ ነው). This is formed with iyye- + verb stem + pronoun suffix + new/nat. Use this for actions happening at this moment.

steps.title: Present vs Future
steps.body: The compound imperfect covers both present habit and future. Context and time words clarify: 'nege ihedallehu' (tomorrow I will go) vs 'hulqen ihedallehu' (I go every day).

title: Negative Forms
description: Learn to negate verbs in past, present, and other constructions

steps.title: Past Negative (al-...-m)
steps.body: To negate the past, wrap the verb with al- and -m: hede (he went) becomes alhede-m (he didn't go). belahu (I ate) becomes albelahu-m (I didn't eat). The 'al...m' frame is the key pattern.

steps.title: Present Negative (ay-...-m)
steps.body: To negate present/future, use ay- and -m: yihedal (he goes) becomes ayhedam (he doesn't go). ibetallehu (I eat) becomes albelam (I don't eat). The prefix changes from al- to ay-.

steps.title: Other Negatives
steps.body: For 'to be': aydelem (አይደለም - is not). For 'to have': yellem (የለም - there is not). For 'don't!' (imperative): athed (don't go m), athedyi (don't go f).

steps.title: Double Negative Warning
steps.body: Unlike English, Amharic does NOT use double negatives. One negation frame is enough. 'Menim albelahum' means 'I didn't eat anything' - 'menim' means 'anything' (not 'nothing').

title: Common Verbs Part 2
description: Expand your verb vocabulary with communication and action verbs

steps.title: Communication Verbs
steps.body: menager (መናገር - to speak/tell), memar (መማር - to learn), maswered (ማስወረድ - to teach), manbeb (ማንበብ - to read), meshaf (መጻፍ - to write).

steps.title: Action Verbs
steps.body: madreg (ማድረግ - to do/make), mewded (መውደድ - to love/like), mawqe (ማወቅ - to know), mesmat (መስማት - to hear/listen), megzat (መግዛት - to buy).

steps.title: Emotional Verbs
steps.body: medeset (መደሰት - to be happy), mazzen (ማዝን - to be sad), mefrat (መፍራት - to fear), memerr (መምረጥ - to choose/prefer).

steps.title: Practice Combinations
steps.body: Combine these verbs with past and present tenses you learned: 'amarigna imerallehu' (I am learning Amharic), 'metshaf anebebhu' (I read a book), 'buna gezahu' (I bought coffee).

title: Imperative & Requests
description: Learn to give commands, make requests, and say please

steps.title: Commands (Gender-Specific)
steps.body: Commands differ by gender. To a man: hid (ሂድ - go!), bla (ብላ - eat!), ta (ጣ - come!). To a woman: hiji (ሂጂ - go!), biyi (ቢይ - eat!), neyi (ነይ - come!). Plural: hidu, bilu, nu.

steps.title: Saying Please
steps.body: To a man: ibakih (እባክህ - please). To a woman: ibakish (እባክሽ - please). Formal: ibakwo (እባክዎ - please). Always use 'ibak-' forms to be polite.

steps.title: Polite Requests
steps.body: Combine 'ibak-' with commands for polite requests: 'ibakih na' (please come, to a man), 'ibakish hinji' (please go, to a woman). You can also use 'ichlaleh?' (can you m?) or 'ichiyalesh?' (can you f?).

steps.title: Negative Commands
steps.body: To say 'don't', add 'at-' before the verb: athid (don't go m), athedyi (don't go f), atbla (don't eat m). These are direct and can sound harsh, so add 'ibak-' for politeness.

title: Introducing Yourself
description: Learn to introduce yourself, share your name, origin, and interests

steps.title: Sharing Your Name
steps.body: To say your name: 'sime ___ yibalal' (my name is called ___). To ask someone's name: 'simih man new?' (what is your name? to m) or 'simish man new?' (to f).

steps.title: Where You're From
steps.body: To share your origin: 'ine ke-Amerika negn' (I am from America). The pattern is 'ke-' + country + 'negn'. To ask: 'ke-yet neh/nesh?' (where are you from?).

steps.title: What You Do
steps.body: To say what you're learning: 'amarigna imerallehu' (I am learning Amharic). To say your job: 'ine memhir negn' (I am a teacher), 'ine temaryi negn' (I am a student).

steps.title: Putting It Together
steps.body: A full introduction: 'Selam! Sime David yibalal. Ine ke-Amerika negn. Amarigna imerallehu.' (Hello! My name is David. I am from America. I am learning Amharic.)

title: At the Market
description: Learn essential phrases for shopping and bargaining at the market

steps.title: Asking Prices
steps.body: To ask 'how much?': 'sint new?' (ስንት ነው?). The seller will respond with a number + birr: 'asir birr' (10 birr). You can also ask 'yih sint new?' (how much is this?).

steps.title: Expressing What You Want
steps.body: 'ifelligallehu' (እፈልጋለሁ - I want). Point and say 'yihin ifelligallehu' (I want this one). 'Alle?' (is there? / do you have?). 'Buna alleh?' (do you have coffee?).

steps.title: Negotiating
steps.body: 'wid new' (ውድ ነው - it's expensive). 'Tinish qennis?' (a little less?). 'Mekakelenya zega?' (middle price?). Bargaining is expected in Ethiopian markets - always negotiate!

steps.title: Completing the Purchase
steps.body: 'Ishi, igezallehu' (OK, I'll buy it). 'Ameseginalehu' (thank you). 'Meles alfelligim' (I don't want change). Being polite goes a long way in Ethiopian markets.

title: Asking for Directions
description: Learn to ask where things are and understand directional words

steps.title: Asking Where
steps.body: 'yet new?' (የት ነው? - where is it?). To ask about a specific place: 'hotel yet new?' (where is the hotel?). 'Yiqirta, bank yet new?' (excuse me, where is the bank?).

steps.title: Directional Words
steps.body: qetita hid (ቀጥታ ሂድ - go straight), wede qegn (ወደ ቀኝ - to the right), wede gra (ወደ ግራ - to the left), ke- ... birr (ከ - near/from), riqqu (ሩቅ - far).

steps.title: Distance & Location
steps.body: 'qirb new' (ቅርብ ነው - it's near). 'ruq new' (ሩቅ ነው - it's far). 'kezih qirb' (near here). 'kebetih fitfit' (in front of your house). Amharic uses postpositions like 'lay' (on), 'wist' (inside).

steps.title: Useful Phrases
steps.body: 'Ameseginalehu, gebahu' (thank you, I found it). 'Algebanyim' (I didn't find it). 'Andegna indigegn' (please help me / show me). Always start with 'yiqirta' to be polite.

title: At a Restaurant
description: Learn to order food and drinks, compliment the food, and pay the bill

steps.title: Ordering Food & Drinks
steps.body: 'migib ifelligallehu' (I want food). 'buna ifelligallehu' (I want coffee). 'menu alleh?' (is there a menu?). 'injera be-wot' (injera with stew - the national dish). Specify: 'doro wot' (chicken stew), 'shiro wot' (chickpea stew).

steps.title: Complimenting the Food
steps.body: 'tiru new!' (ጥሩ ነው - it's good!). 'betam tiru new!' (it's very good!). 'yeteshetegn' (I'm full). Ethiopians love when guests enjoy the food, so always compliment the meal.

steps.title: Getting the Bill
steps.body: 'hisabu' (ሂሳቡ - the bill). 'hisabu ibakih' (the bill please, to m). 'sint new?' (how much is it?). Tipping is appreciated but not mandatory - around 10% is customary.

steps.title: Coffee Ceremony
steps.body: Ethiopian coffee ceremony is a cultural treasure. 'Buna tetchetwalech' (coffee has been brewed). You typically drink 3 cups: abol (first), tona (second), bereka (third - the blessing). Never refuse coffee - it's a sign of respect.

title: Formal vs Informal
description: Learn when and how to switch between formal and informal speech

steps.title: When to Use Formal
steps.body: Use formal speech with: elders, strangers, people in authority, in-laws, religious leaders. Use informal with: friends, peers, children, close family. When in doubt, go formal - it's always safer and more respectful.

steps.title: Formal Pronouns & Titles
steps.body: 'irswo' (እርስዎ) replaces 'ante/anchi' in formal speech. Titles: 'Ato' (አቶ - Mr.), 'Weyzero' (ወይዘሮ - Mrs.), 'Weyzerit' (ወይዘሪት - Miss). Always use titles with elders.

steps.title: Polite Verb Forms
steps.body: Formal conjugations use the 'irswo' form: 'indet nachiw?' (how are you? formal), 'yimtu' (come, formal command). Adding 'ibakih/ibakish/ibakwo' (please) makes any request more polite.

steps.title: Polite Expressions
steps.body: 'minem aydelem' (ምነም አይደለም - you're welcome / it's nothing). 'yiqirta' (ይቅርታ - excuse me/sorry). 'abet!' (አቤት - an exclamation to get attention politely).

title: Daily Conversation Practice
description: Practice complete dialogues combining everything you've learned

steps.title: Review Key Patterns
steps.body: By now you know: greetings, pronouns, SOV order, past/present tenses, questions, and polite forms. Real conversation combines all of these. Practice flowing between patterns naturally.

steps.title: Sample Greeting Dialogue
steps.body: A: 'Selam! Indet neh?' B: 'Dehna negn, ameseginalehu. Ante indet neh?' A: 'Ine dehna. Zare min tiseraleh?' B: 'Sira ihedallehu.' This covers greeting, asking how someone is, and saying where you're going.

steps.title: Sample Market Dialogue
steps.body: You: 'Yiqirta, yih sint new?' Seller: 'Hamsa birr.' You: 'Wid new! Selasa birr?' Seller: 'Arba birr, yimeTash.' You: 'Ishi, igezallehu.' This covers asking price, negotiating, and buying.

steps.title: Tips for Fluency
steps.body: Speak slowly at first, Ethiopians will appreciate the effort. Use 'yiqirta' often. Don't fear mistakes - people love that you're trying. Listen to Amharic music and shows. Practice greetings with every interaction.
